content.explanation:
Lazy loading is a performance optimization technique that defers the loading of non-critical resources until they are actually needed. Instead of loading every image, iframe, and media element when the page first loads, lazy loading waits until these elements are about to enter the viewport before fetching them. This dramatically reduces initial page load time, saves bandwidth for users who never scroll to certain content, and decreases server load by spreading resource requests over time.

Native lazy loading in HTML is achieved through the loading attribute, which accepts three values: 'lazy', 'eager', and 'auto'. Setting loading="lazy" on an img or iframe element tells the browser to defer loading until the element is near the viewport. The browser handles all the complexity internally, including calculating distance thresholds based on network conditions and device capabilities. This is the simplest approach and requires zero JavaScript, but it is only supported for img and iframe elements and offers limited control over the loading threshold.

The Intersection Observer API provides a JavaScript-based approach to lazy loading with far more control. You create an IntersectionObserver instance with a callback and options (root, rootMargin, threshold), then observe target elements. When an observed element intersects with the root (viewport by default), the callback fires and you can swap a placeholder src with the real image URL stored in a data attribute. This approach works for any element type, supports custom thresholds and root margins for pre-loading content before it enters the viewport, and can trigger animations or other effects based on visibility.

Before Intersection Observer, developers relied on scroll event listeners with getBoundingClientRect() to detect element positions. This older approach is significantly less performant because scroll events fire at a very high rate and getBoundingClientRect() forces layout recalculation (reflow). The Intersection Observer API was specifically designed to replace this pattern, running asynchronously off the main thread and batching intersection calculations efficiently. Always prefer Intersection Observer over scroll-based detection in modern code.

A comprehensive lazy loading strategy considers several factors: above-the-fold images should use loading="eager" or have no loading attribute to ensure they load immediately. Below-the-fold images use loading="lazy". Critical resources like hero images, logos, and LCP (Largest Contentful Paint) elements should never be lazy loaded. For background images set via CSS, Intersection Observer is the only option since the loading attribute does not apply to CSS properties. Placeholder strategies include low-quality image placeholders (LQIP), solid color blocks, skeleton screens, or blur-up effects that provide visual feedback while the full image loads.

codeExamples.title: Native lazy loading with the loading attribute
codeExamples.explanation: The loading attribute is the simplest way to implement lazy loading. Set loading='eager' for above-the-fold critical images and loading='lazy' for everything else. Always include width and height attributes to reserve space and prevent Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS). The decoding='async' attribute allows the browser to decode the image off the main thread.

codeExamples.title: Intersection Observer-based lazy loading
codeExamples.explanation: This approach stores the real image URL in data-src and uses a lightweight placeholder in src. The IntersectionObserver watches each image and swaps the source when the image is 200px from entering the viewport. After loading, the observer stops watching that image to free resources.

codeExamples.title: Lazy loading with blur-up placeholder effect
codeExamples.explanation: The blur-up technique starts with a tiny base64-encoded placeholder that is displayed blurred. When the full image loads, the blur filter is removed with a CSS transition, creating a smooth reveal effect. The Image() constructor preloads the full image in the background so the transition only starts when the image is fully downloaded.

notes: Native loading='lazy' is supported in all modern browsers (Chrome, Firefox, Edge, Safari 15.4+). Check caniuse.com for current support.
Never lazy load the Largest Contentful Paint (LCP) element -- it must load as fast as possible for Core Web Vitals.
Always provide width and height on lazy loaded images to prevent Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS).
The rootMargin option in IntersectionObserver lets you pre-load images before they enter the viewport, creating a seamless scrolling experience.
commonPitfalls: Lazy loading above-the-fold or hero images, which delays the Largest Contentful Paint and hurts performance scores.
Forgetting to set width and height on lazy images, causing layout shifts when the image finally loads.
Using scroll event listeners instead of Intersection Observer, which causes jank due to high-frequency event firing and forced reflows.
Not providing a fallback for environments where JavaScript is disabled -- the image will never load if src only contains a placeholder.
interviewTips: Explain the difference between native lazy loading (loading attribute) and JavaScript-based (Intersection Observer) approaches, and when each is appropriate.
Mention that lazy loading improves three Core Web Vitals metrics: LCP (by prioritizing critical images), CLS (with proper dimensions), and FID/INP (by reducing main thread work).
Be ready to implement an Intersection Observer from scratch and explain the options: root, rootMargin, and threshold.

exercises.title: Implement Native Lazy Loading
exercises.description: Create an HTML page with a hero image that loads eagerly and a gallery of 6 images below the fold that use native lazy loading. Each image must include proper width, height, alt text, and the decoding='async' attribute. Use meaningful alt text for each image.
exercises.starterCode: Photo Gallery
exercises.solution: Photo Gallery
hints: Use loading='eager' on the hero image and loading='lazy' on gallery images.
Always include width and height attributes to reserve space and prevent layout shifts.

exercises.title: Build an Intersection Observer Lazy Loader
exercises.description: Write a JavaScript function called createLazyLoader that uses the Intersection Observer API to lazy load images. The function should accept a CSS selector string and an optional rootMargin value (defaulting to '100px 0px'). It should find all matching images, observe them, and swap their data-src attribute into src when they become visible. After swapping, the observer should stop watching the image and add a 'loaded' CSS class.
hints: Use document.querySelectorAll(selector) to find the images, then iterate with forEach to observe each one.
In the observer callback, check entry.isIntersecting before swapping the source. Call observer.unobserve(entry.target) after swapping to stop watching.

content.explanation:
Resource hints are HTML mechanisms that allow developers to inform the browser about resources it will need in the near future, enabling the browser to start fetching or preparing connections before they are actually required. The four primary resource hints are preload, prefetch, preconnect, and dns-prefetch. Each serves a different purpose and operates at a different priority level, and using them correctly can significantly improve perceived and actual page load performance.

Preload (link rel='preload') tells the browser to fetch a specific resource immediately with high priority because it is needed for the current page. Unlike normal resource discovery, which happens as the browser parses HTML and encounters references, preload forces the browser to start downloading a resource before it would naturally discover it. This is critical for resources referenced in CSS (like fonts and background images) or loaded via JavaScript, which the browser cannot discover until it has parsed and executed those files. Preload requires the 'as' attribute to specify the resource type (font, image, script, style, fetch) so the browser can apply correct caching headers and Content Security Policy checks.

Prefetch (link rel='prefetch') is a low-priority hint that tells the browser to fetch a resource that will likely be needed for a future navigation. The browser downloads prefetched resources during idle time, so they do not compete with current page resources. This is ideal for pre-loading the next page's JavaScript bundle, CSS, or data when you can predict where the user will navigate next. Prefetch is purely speculative and browsers may ignore it if the device is on a slow connection, has limited memory, or data saver mode is enabled.

Preconnect (link rel='preconnect') instructs the browser to establish an early connection to a specified origin, completing the DNS lookup, TCP handshake, and TLS negotiation before any request is made. This eliminates the connection setup latency (often 100-500ms) when the actual request occurs. Preconnect is most valuable for third-party origins like CDNs, font services, analytics endpoints, and API servers. Use it sparingly -- each preconnect maintains an open connection that consumes resources, so limit it to 2-4 critical origins.

DNS-prefetch (link rel='dns-prefetch') is a lighter version of preconnect that only performs the DNS lookup step. It resolves a domain name to an IP address, which typically takes 20-120ms. DNS-prefetch has wider browser support than preconnect and is appropriate for origins where you want to reduce latency but the connection is not critical enough to warrant a full preconnect. It is common to use both as a progressive enhancement: set preconnect for modern browsers and dns-prefetch as a fallback for older ones.

The fetchpriority attribute (available on img, script, link, and iframe elements) complements resource hints by letting you adjust the priority of individual resources. Setting fetchpriority='high' on the LCP image ensures it loads before other images. Setting fetchpriority='low' on non-critical scripts prevents them from competing with important resources. Combining fetchpriority with preload gives you precise control over what loads first and at what priority, which is essential for optimizing Core Web Vitals.

codeExamples.title: Preload critical resources
codeExamples.code:
<head>
  <!-- Preload a font file (must include crossorigin for fonts) -->
  <link
    rel="preload"
    href="/fonts/Inter-Regular.woff2"
    as="font"
    type="font/woff2"
    crossorigin="anonymous"
  />

  <!-- Preload the hero image for faster LCP -->
  <link
    rel="preload"
    href="/images/hero-banner.webp"
    as="image"
    type="image/webp"
  />

  <!-- Preload a critical CSS file -->
  <link
    rel="preload"
    href="/css/critical.css"
    as="style"
  />
  <link rel="stylesheet" href="/css/critical.css" />

  <!-- Preload a JavaScript module -->
  <link
    rel="modulepreload"
    href="/js/app.mjs"
  />
</head>
codeExamples.explanation: Preload forces the browser to start downloading critical resources immediately. Fonts require the crossorigin attribute even for same-origin requests. The 'as' attribute is mandatory and must match the resource type for correct prioritization. Use modulepreload for ES modules instead of preload with as='script'.

codeExamples.title: Prefetch resources for future navigation
codeExamples.code: Dashboard Settings
codeExamples.explanation: Prefetch loads resources with low priority during idle time. The dynamic prefetch pattern creates link elements on hover, giving the browser a head start on loading the next page's resources. The { once: true } option ensures each link is only prefetched once.

codeExamples.title: Preconnect and DNS-prefetch for third-party origins
codeExamples.code:
<head>
  <!-- Preconnect to Google Fonts (DNS + TCP + TLS) -->
  <link rel="preconnect" href="https://fonts.googleapis.com" />
  <link
    rel="preconnect"
    href="https://fonts.gstatic.com"
    crossorigin="anonymous"
  />

  <!-- Preconnect to your API server -->
  <link rel="preconnect" href="https://api.example.com" />

  <!-- DNS-prefetch as fallback for older browsers -->
  <link rel="dns-prefetch" href="https://fonts.googleapis.com" />
  <link rel="dns-prefetch" href="https://fonts.gstatic.com" />

  <!-- DNS-prefetch for analytics (not critical enough for preconnect) -->
  <link rel="dns-prefetch" href="https://analytics.example.com" />
  <link rel="dns-prefetch" href="https://cdn.example.com" />
</head>
codeExamples.explanation: Preconnect eliminates connection setup latency for critical third-party origins. Google Fonts requires preconnect to two origins: googleapis.com serves the CSS, gstatic.com serves the font files. DNS-prefetch is used as a fallback and for less critical origins where a full preconnect would waste resources.

codeExamples.title: Priority hints with fetchpriority
codeExamples.explanation: The fetchpriority attribute overrides the browser's default resource priority. Set 'high' on LCP images and critical scripts, 'low' on non-essential resources. This works on img, script, link, and iframe elements. Combining fetchpriority='high' with preload ensures the LCP image loads as fast as possible.

notes: Preload resources that are needed within 3 seconds of page load. Browsers log console warnings for preloaded resources not used within this window.
Fonts must include crossorigin='anonymous' in preload tags even when served from the same origin, due to how font requests work.
Limit preconnect to 2-4 critical origins. Each open connection consumes browser and server resources.
Use the Network tab in DevTools to identify resources that would benefit from preloading by looking at their discovery time vs download start time.
commonPitfalls: Preloading too many resources, which competes for bandwidth and can actually slow down the page instead of speeding it up.
Forgetting the 'as' attribute on preload links, which causes the browser to download the resource twice or apply incorrect priority.
Using preload for resources needed on future pages instead of prefetch -- preload is high priority and intended for the current page only.
Not including crossorigin on font preload tags, which causes a double download because the preloaded resource cannot be matched to the font request.
interviewTips: Clearly distinguish preload (current page, high priority) from prefetch (future pages, low priority, idle time).
Explain the connection establishment steps that preconnect skips: DNS lookup, TCP handshake, TLS negotiation.
Mention that preload requires the 'as' attribute and explain why (resource type determines priority, CSP checks, and caching).

exercises.title: Optimize a Page Head with Resource Hints
exercises.description: You have a page that loads a Google Font, a hero image, a critical CSS file, and makes API calls to api.example.com. Rewrite the <head> section with appropriate preload, preconnect, and dns-prefetch tags. Also add a prefetch for the next page's JavaScript bundle (/js/next-page.js).
hints: Google Fonts requires preconnect to both fonts.googleapis.com (CSS) and fonts.gstatic.com (font files, needs crossorigin).
Place resource hints before the resources they optimize. Use preload for current-page critical resources and prefetch for the next page's bundle.

exercises.title: Dynamic Prefetch on Link Hover
exercises.description: Write a JavaScript function called setupPrefetchOnHover that finds all anchor tags with a data-prefetch attribute. When the user hovers over any of these links, dynamically create a <link rel='prefetch'> element for that link's href and append it to the document head. Ensure each link is only prefetched once (do not add duplicate prefetch tags). Return the total number of links being observed.
exercises.starterCode: Dashboard Profile Settings
exercises.solution: Dashboard Profile Settings
hints: Use a Set to track which hrefs have already been prefetched to prevent duplicates.
Use { once: true } as the third argument to addEventListener so the listener is automatically removed after the first hover.

content.explanation:
The Critical Rendering Path (CRP) is the sequence of steps the browser performs to convert HTML, CSS, and JavaScript into pixels on the screen. Understanding this pipeline is essential for optimizing page load performance and is one of the most frequently asked topics in frontend interviews. The CRP consists of five main steps: DOM construction, CSSOM construction, render tree assembly, layout (reflow), and paint (repaint), followed by compositing.

DOM construction begins when the browser receives raw HTML bytes from the server. It converts these bytes into characters (based on encoding), tokenizes them into HTML tokens (start tags, end tags, text content), and converts tokens into DOM nodes organized in a tree structure. This process is incremental -- the browser starts building the DOM as soon as it receives the first chunk of HTML and does not wait for the entire document. However, when the parser encounters a <script> tag without async or defer, it must pause DOM construction, download the script, and execute it before continuing, because JavaScript can modify the DOM via document.write() or other APIs.

CSS Object Model (CSSOM) construction follows a similar process: CSS bytes are converted to characters, tokenized, parsed into nodes, and assembled into a tree. Unlike DOM construction, CSSOM construction is render-blocking -- the browser cannot render anything until the entire CSSOM is built because CSS rules can override each other and the final computed styles depend on the complete stylesheet. This is why CSS is called a render-blocking resource. The browser must wait for all CSS to be downloaded and parsed before it can proceed to the next step.

The render tree is constructed by combining the DOM and CSSOM. The browser walks the DOM tree and for each visible node, finds and applies the matching CSSOM rules. Nodes that are not visible (elements with display:none, <head> content, <script> tags) are excluded from the render tree. Note that elements with visibility:hidden are included in the render tree because they still occupy space -- only display:none removes elements entirely. The render tree contains only the information needed for rendering: each node has its content and computed styles.

Layout (also called reflow) is the process where the browser calculates the exact position and size of each render tree node. Starting from the root, it traverses the tree and computes the geometry (position, width, height, margins) based on the viewport size, box model rules, and CSS properties. Layout is expensive because changing one element's geometry can affect its parent, siblings, and children -- a change can cascade through the entire tree. Repaint occurs when visual properties change without affecting layout (color, background-color, box-shadow, visibility). Repaint is cheaper than reflow but still not free.

Reflow and repaint are triggered by various operations and understanding what triggers each is crucial for performance. Properties that change geometry (width, height, margin, padding, position, font-size) trigger reflow followed by repaint. Properties that only change appearance (color, background, border-color, outline) trigger only repaint. Reading layout properties (offsetWidth, getBoundingClientRect(), scrollTop) can force a synchronous layout if the DOM has been modified since the last layout, creating a pattern called 'forced synchronous layout' or 'layout thrashing'. Compositing, the final step, combines painted layers into the final image. Transforms and opacity changes can be handled entirely on the GPU compositor thread without triggering layout or paint, which is why transform-based animations are far more performant than animating left/top.

codeExamples.title: Render-blocking CSS and parser-blocking JavaScript
codeExamples.code:
Hello World

This content waits for CSS + blocking JS.
codeExamples.explanation: CSS files are render-blocking (the browser cannot render until CSSOM is complete). Scripts without async/defer are parser-blocking (they stop DOM construction). The defer attribute downloads scripts in parallel but executes them in order after the DOM is fully parsed. The async attribute downloads in parallel and executes immediately when ready, regardless of DOM state.

codeExamples.title: Layout thrashing and how to fix it
codeExamples.explanation: Layout thrashing occurs when you interleave DOM reads and writes in a loop. Each read forces the browser to recalculate layout to return accurate values. The fix is to separate reads and writes into distinct phases: first read all values, then write all changes. Using requestAnimationFrame for writes ensures changes are batched before the next repaint.

codeExamples.title: Properties that trigger reflow vs repaint vs compositing
codeExamples.explanation: Understanding which CSS properties trigger which rendering phases is key to writing performant CSS. Composite-only properties (transform, opacity) are the cheapest to animate because they run on the GPU without triggering layout or paint. Always prefer transform-based animations over top/left/width/height animations.

codeExamples.title: Optimizing the Critical Rendering Path
codeExamples.code:
Welcome

This content renders without waiting for external CSS or JS.

Rest of the page loads progressively.
codeExamples.explanation: This example shows four CRP optimizations: (1) inline critical CSS eliminates the render-blocking stylesheet request for above-the-fold content, (2) the full stylesheet is loaded asynchronously via preload with a rel swap, (3) preconnect eliminates connection latency for API calls, and (4) JavaScript is deferred to avoid blocking DOM construction.

notes: The browser builds the DOM incrementally but the CSSOM must be fully constructed before rendering. This is why CSS is render-blocking and should be delivered as fast as possible.
display:none removes an element from the render tree entirely (no layout or paint cost). visibility:hidden keeps it in the render tree (it still takes up space and costs layout).
The 'will-change' CSS property hints to the browser that an element will be animated, causing it to be promoted to its own compositor layer. Use it sparingly -- too many layers consume GPU memory.
Chrome DevTools Performance tab visualizes the rendering pipeline. The purple bars represent layout (reflow), green bars represent paint, and you can see compositing in the Layers panel.
commonPitfalls: Placing render-blocking CSS at the bottom of <head> or in <body>, which delays CSSOM construction and pushes back first paint.
Layout thrashing by alternating DOM reads (offsetWidth, getBoundingClientRect) and writes (style changes) in a loop, forcing multiple synchronous layouts per frame.
Animating layout properties like width, height, top, and left instead of using transform, which triggers expensive reflows on every animation frame.
Using will-change on too many elements or leaving it permanently set, which creates excessive compositor layers and wastes GPU memory.
interviewTips: Walk through the CRP step by step: bytes -> characters -> tokens -> nodes -> DOM/CSSOM -> render tree -> layout -> paint -> composite.
Explain why CSS is render-blocking but not parser-blocking, while JavaScript (without defer/async) is parser-blocking.
Describe the difference between reflow and repaint, give examples of properties that trigger each, and explain why transform/opacity are cheap to animate.
Mention that reading layout properties after DOM writes causes forced synchronous layout and how to fix it with read/write batching.

exercises.title: Identify Rendering Issues
exercises.description: The following HTML has several performance problems related to the Critical Rendering Path. Rewrite it to fix the issues: move render-blocking resources appropriately, defer JavaScript, inline critical CSS, and async-load the full stylesheet. The page has a hero heading and a paragraph as above-the-fold content.
exercises.starterCode:
Welcome to My Site

Above the fold content here.

Below the fold content
exercises.solution:
Welcome to My Site

Above the fold content here.

Below the fold content
hints: Scripts without defer or async block DOM parsing. Move them to <head> with defer so they download in parallel and execute after the DOM is built.
CSS in <body> causes a flash of unstyled content. Inline critical CSS in <head> and async-load the full stylesheet.

exercises.title: Fix Layout Thrashing
exercises.description: The following JavaScript code causes layout thrashing by interleaving reads and writes in a loop. Refactor it to batch all reads first, then batch all writes. The code resizes a list of elements to be twice their current width and sets their height to match their text content height.
exercises.starterCode:
Short text
Some longer text content here
Medium text
exercises.solution:
Short text
Some longer text content here
Medium text
hints: Separate the loop into two phases: first loop reads all values into an array, second loop writes all changes.
Wrap the write phase in requestAnimationFrame to batch the DOM writes before the next repaint.

content.explanation:
HTML validation is the process of checking whether your HTML markup conforms to the standards defined by the W3C (World Wide Web Consortium). Valid HTML ensures consistent rendering across browsers, improves accessibility, helps search engines parse your content correctly, and reduces unexpected behavior caused by browser error-correction algorithms. The W3C Markup Validation Service (validator.w3.org) is the official tool for checking HTML validity, and understanding common validation errors is essential for writing professional-quality markup. The DOCTYPE declaration is the very first line of an HTML document and tells the browser which version of HTML the page uses. In HTML5, the declaration is simply -- this triggers standards mode in all browsers. Without a DOCTYPE or with an incorrect one, browsers fall into quirks mode, where they emulate legacy rendering behavior from the late 1990s. In quirks mode, the box model calculates differently (width includes padding and border), table cells inherit font sizes differently, inline elements behave differently, and many CSS features do not work as expected. Always start every HTML document with . Common HTML validation errors fall into several categories. Structural errors include unclosed tags, improperly nested elements (like

where a block element is inside an inline element), duplicate IDs on a page, and missing required attributes (like alt on img elements). Content model violations occur when elements are placed where the HTML spec does not allow them, such as block-level elements inside

tags or interactive elements nested inside other interactive elements (a link inside a button). Attribute errors include using deprecated attributes, misspelled attribute names, and invalid attribute values. Beyond the W3C validator, several other validation tools help ensure markup quality. Browser DevTools display parsing errors in the console. The axe accessibility checker and WAVE tool validate accessibility-related markup issues. HTML linters like htmlhint and html-validate can be integrated into your build process for automated checking. ESLint with the jsx-a11y plugin validates JSX accessibility in React projects. Running validation as part of your CI/CD pipeline catches errors before they reach production. Valid HTML is not just about passing a validator -- it has real impact on user experience and business metrics. Screen readers rely on valid, semantic HTML to convey content structure to visually impaired users. Search engine crawlers may misinterpret or penalize pages with significant markup errors. Invalid HTML can cause layout differences across browsers because each browser has its own error-recovery algorithm. Even seemingly minor issues like unclosed tags can cause cascading layout problems where styles intended for one section leak into others.

codeExamples.title: DOCTYPE and its effect on rendering mode
codeExamples.code:
This page renders in standards mode.

Quirks Mode

This page renders in quirks mode!
codeExamples.explanation: The <!DOCTYPE html> declaration must be the very first line of the document (no whitespace or comments before it). It triggers standards mode rendering. document.compatMode returns 'CSS1Compat' for standards mode and 'BackCompat' for quirks mode.

codeExamples.title: Common validation errors and fixes
codeExamples.code:
First header
Second header
First header
Second header
Block inside inline
Block inside block

Some text

This breaks the paragraph

Some text

This is separate
Centered text
Red text
Centered text
Red text
codeExamples.explanation: IDs must be unique per page. Block elements cannot be nested inside inline elements. The alt attribute is required on all img elements (use empty alt='' for decorative images). Elements like <center> and <font> are obsolete and should be replaced with CSS.

codeExamples.title: Proper document structure for validation
codeExamples.code:
• Home
  • About

Page Title

Article Title

Article content with bold text.

© 2026 Company Name
codeExamples.explanation: A valid HTML5 document requires: DOCTYPE declaration, html element with lang attribute, head with charset meta and title, and a body. Using semantic elements (header, nav, main, article, footer, figure) improves both validation scores and accessibility. Only one h1 per page is recommended for SEO.

notes: The W3C validator at validator.w3.org can validate by URL, file upload, or direct HTML input. It reports errors, warnings, and informational messages.

Browsers are extremely forgiving of HTML errors -- they silently fix missing closing tags, reorder misplaced elements, and autocorrect attributes. This error recovery is why invalid HTML often appears to work fine.
The lang attribute on <html> is not just for validation -- it enables correct text-to-speech pronunciation, hyphenation, and spell-checking.
Run 'npx html-validate your-file.html' for command-line validation that can be integrated into build scripts and CI/CD pipelines.
commonPitfalls: Omitting the DOCTYPE declaration, which silently puts the browser into quirks mode and causes subtle rendering differences across browsers.
Using duplicate IDs on a page, which causes JavaScript getElementById to return only the first match and breaks accessibility for screen readers.
Nesting interactive elements inside each other (link inside button, button inside link), which is invalid HTML and causes unpredictable click behavior across browsers.
Relying on browser error correction instead of writing valid HTML -- different browsers correct errors differently, leading to cross-browser inconsistencies.
interviewTips: Know what DOCTYPE does and what happens without it (quirks mode vs standards mode). Be able to explain document.compatMode.
List common validation errors: duplicate IDs, missing alt, block in inline, missing DOCTYPE, obsolete elements.
Explain why valid HTML matters beyond just passing a validator: accessibility, SEO, cross-browser consistency, and maintainability.

exercises.title: Fix Invalid HTML
exercises.description: The following HTML document has at least 8 validation errors. Find and fix all of them. Errors include missing DOCTYPE, duplicate IDs, missing required attributes, improper nesting, obsolete elements, and structural issues.
exercises.starterCode:
Home About

Welcome

Click to start.

Duplicate ID section

Block inside paragraph

Centered text
Important notice
exercises.solution:
Home About

Welcome

Click here to start.

Previously duplicate ID section

Content now properly structured

Centered text

Important notice
hints: Count the issues: missing DOCTYPE, missing lang, missing charset, button inside anchor, missing alt, duplicate ID, div inside p, obsolete center, obsolete font.
Replace obsolete elements with CSS equivalents. Fix nesting issues by restructuring the HTML hierarchy.

exercises.title: Build a Valid HTML5 Document from Scratch
exercises.description: Create a complete, valid HTML5 document for a blog post page. Include: DOCTYPE, lang attribute, charset meta, viewport meta, title, a semantic structure with header (containing nav with 3 links), main (with an article containing h1, two paragraphs, an image with proper attributes, and a figure with figcaption), and a footer with copyright. Every element must use proper nesting and all required attributes.
exercises.starterCode:
<!-- Write a complete, valid HTML5 blog post page -->
<!-- Requirements:
  - DOCTYPE, html with lang, head with charset + viewport + title
  - header with nav (3 links in a list)
  - main with article (h1, 2 paragraphs, image, figure/figcaption)
  - footer with copyright
  - All required attributes present
  - No validation errors
-->
exercises.solution:
• Home
  • Blog
  • Contact

Understanding CSS Grid

CSS Grid Layout is a two-dimensional layout system designed for the web. It allows you to organize content into rows and columns simultaneously, making it ideal for complex page layouts that were previously difficult to achieve.

Unlike Flexbox, which is primarily one-dimensional, Grid gives you full control over both axes at once. You can define explicit tracks, create template areas, and build responsive layouts with minimal media queries.

© 2026 My Blog. All rights reserved.
hints: Start with <!DOCTYPE html> and add lang='en' to the html element. Include both charset and viewport meta tags in the head.
Use semantic elements: header, nav, main, article, figure, figcaption, footer. Ensure the img has src, alt, width, and height attributes.
